CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, with the main contributions stated early and clearly (DFP approach, multidimensional measurements, goal-conditioning). Notation and definitions appear consistent, and figures/tables (scenarios, baselines, metrics like health/frags, training speed) effectively support the text; hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Writing quality is good overall, though a few sections (e.g., limitations and experimental protocols like seed/variance reporting and code availability) could be more explicit to aid reproducibility and remove minor remaining ambiguities. | B(4): From the provided summary the paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early and repeatedly (DFP, use of vectorial measurements, goal-conditioning). Notation and methodological description are reported as consistent and related work is covered thoroughly; figures/tables (scenarios, baselines, metrics) seem informative and the writing quality is good overall. Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., explicit mention of code or appendices for reproducibility is absent), but clarity and flow are strong, so I rate it a 4.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable new perspective by replacing reward-driven RL with supervised direct future prediction (DFP) of dense, vectorial measurements and using those predictions for goal-conditioned action selection — this is a clear methodological shift from DQN/A3C/DSR baselines. The formulation of predicting multidimensional measurement streams at multiple horizons and decoupling training from fixed goals is a substantive, non-trivial extension beyond standard value- or policy-based methods. The authors explicitly differentiate their contributions from prior RL approaches and demonstrate empirical advantages on challenging ViZDoom scenarios, so the work would be informative and somewhat surprising to experts, though it builds on existing prediction- and model-based ideas rather than introducing an entirely unprecedented paradigm. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly different perspective from standard value- or policy-learning by framing control as supervised prediction of multidimensional future measurements conditioned on candidate actions and goal vectors (DFP), which is a notable departure from canonical RL algorithms evaluated (DQN, A3C, DSR). Using dense measurement vectors and test-time goal specification is a meaningful conceptual shift beyond scalar reward prediction and connects to, but is distinct from, prior ideas like UVFA or model-based prediction. The technique is therefore a non-trivial and informative contribution for experts, though it builds on familiar building blocks (deep nets for prediction/action selection) rather than introducing entirely new theoretical machinery.

The formulation of predicting multidimensional measurement streams at multiple horizons and decoupling training from fixed goals is a substantive, non-trivial extension beyond standard value- or policy-based methods. The authors explicitly differentiate their contributions from prior RL approaches and demonstrate empirical advantages on challenging ViZDoom scenarios, so the work would be informative and somewhat surprising to experts, though it builds on existing prediction- and model-based ideas rather than introducing an entirely unprecedented paradigm.

Using dense measurement vectors and test-time goal specification is a meaningful conceptual shift beyond scalar reward prediction and connects to, but is distinct from, prior ideas like UVFA or model-based prediction. The technique is therefore a non-trivial and informative contribution for experts, though it builds on familiar building blocks (deep nets for prediction/action selection) rather than introducing entirely new theoretical machinery.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by targeted experiments comparing DFP to DQN/A3C/DSR on multiple ViZDoom scenarios (including multitexture variants) with sensible metrics (health, frags, training speed) and reported hyperparameters. However, important reproducibility/statistical details (e.g., number of random seeds, variance/error bars, code release) and whether baselines were tuned to the same budget are not specified in the summary, leaving some uncertainty about significance and fairness. The methodology (supervised prediction of future measurements and action selection) is plausible and no mathematical errors are indicated, but the reliance on a purely reactive model and discrete actions is an acknowledged limitation that narrows the claim scope. Overall the evidence supports the main conclusions but with minor gaps in statistical rigor and reproducibility. | B(4): The paper’s main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments across multiple ViZDoom scenarios (including multitexture variants) and an external evaluation (Visual Doom AI Competition), with comparisons to relevant baselines (DQN, A3C, DSR) and reported training speed and hyperparameters. There are no formal mathematical derivations to verify, and the approach is evaluated empirically which is appropriate; however the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or variance reporting, and the reactive/no-memory design is an acknowledged limitation that constrains some claims about generalization in partially observable settings. Overall the evidence substantiates the core empirical claims but minor methodological details (e.g., statistical analysis, handling partial observability) are missing, so I deduct one point.

However, important reproducibility/statistical details (e.g., number of random seeds, variance/error bars, code release) and whether baselines were tuned to the same budget are not specified in the summary, leaving some uncertainty about significance and fairness. The methodology (supervised prediction of future measurements and action selection) is plausible and no mathematical errors are indicated, but the reliance on a purely reactive model and discrete actions is an acknowledged limitation that narrows the claim scope. Overall the evidence supports the main conclusions but with minor gaps in statistical rigor and reproducibility.

There are no formal mathematical derivations to verify, and the approach is evaluated empirically which is appropriate; however the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or variance reporting, and the reactive/no-memory design is an acknowledged limitation that constrains some claims about generalization in partially observable settings. Overall the evidence substantiates the core empirical claims but minor methodological details (e.g., statistical analysis, handling partial observability) are missing, so I deduct one point.

main_claims: Direct future prediction (DFP), a supervised-learning approach that predicts future measurements from sensory input and goals, outperforms state-of-the-art deep reinforcement learning methods on challenging sensorimotor tasks in 3D environments.
Using dense, vectorial (multidimensional) measurement streams as supervision yields better performance than using a scalar reward and improves learning and generalization.
The learned models can be trained without a fixed goal and can pursue dynamically specified goals at test time, generalizing across environments and goals.
_summary.methodology: Train a deep network to predict future measurement differences at multiple temporal offsets conditioned on current sensory input, measurements, a candidate action, and a goal vector; at test time select the action whose predicted future measurements maximize the goal objective.
datasets: ViZDoom scenarios (D1 Basic, D2 Navigation, D3 Battle, D4 Battle 2) including multitexture variants D3-tx and D4-tx; Visual Doom AI Competition (Full Deathmatch) evaluation
metrics: Average health at end of episode
Average frags at end of episode
Training speed (millions of simulation steps per day)
limitations: Model is purely reactive (no explicit memory) as presented.
Model developed for discrete action spaces; continuous actions not addressed.
Predicting full sensory input (rather than selected measurements) is acknowledged as beyond current capabilities.
_summary.topic_area: Reinforcement learning / sensorimotor control from raw sensory input in 3D simulated environments

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML researcher and engineer with a decade of experience building and evaluating deep learning systems for embodied agents and simulated environments; has led reproducible benchmarking efforts and open-source implementations for RL and supervised prediction methods.
traits: insists on clearly specified experimental protocols, hyperparameters, compute budgets, and random seeds to assess reproducibility
values comprehensive baselines and ablations (e.g., architecture choices, horizon choices, measurement dimensionality, training curricula) to isolate the source of improvements
checks statistical rigor: multiple random seeds, significance or variability reporting, and evaluation on held-out environments
looks for implementation details that affect fairness of comparisons (frame-skip, frame stacking, action repeats, reward shaping, optimiser tuning, data augmentation)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and analyses support the empirical claims and whether the work can be reproduced and fairly compared to prior methods.

reviewer_b.name: Embodied Cognition and Control Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Professor in robotics and reinforcement learning with research spanning sensorimotor control from visual inputs, goal-conditioned policies, and predictive representations for planning in 3D simulated and real environments.
traits: evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior predictive modeling and goal-conditioned RL work (e.g., model-based RL, value prediction networks, successor features, Universal Value Function Approximators)
assesses practical significance: whether the approach scales, generalizes to new goals/environments, and transfers to downstream robotic or interactive tasks

notices conceptual clarity about what is learned (policy vs. model vs. value), the role of measurement vectors vs. scalar rewards, and limitations regarding partial observability or long-horizon planning
values discussion of failure modes, interpretability of learned predictions, and connections to broader cognitive/robotic principles
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes where the contribution sits in the research landscape, its novelty and practical impact, and whether the conceptual claims are well-motivated and substantiated.
